segments.s148-g17: سخن هرچه پرسم is a relative/nominal phrase 'the words/whatsoever I ask' with پرسم (first‑person present/subjunctive form) qualifying سخن. همه راست گوی is an imperative/subjunctive exhortation addressed to the addressee: همه here means 'entirely/wholly' and راست گوی 'be truthful / speak truly' (گوی is a deverbal noun/saying). The clause thus links as a request: 'Whatever words I ask, speak wholly truthfully.'
segments.s148-g18: متاب is a negative exhortation ('do not waver/turn aside') addressed to the second person; از ره راستی is a prepositional phrase 'from the path/way of truth/honesty' (ره = way, راستی = truth/honesty). هیچ روی is an emphatic negation 'do not in any way turn' (هیچ reinforcing the prohibition). Together: 'Do not turn away from the path of honesty at all.'
segments.s148-g19: چو خواهی که یابی رهایی ز من is a conditional clause: چو...خواهی 'if you wish' (چو used as 'if'), که introduces the content clause, and یابی (subjunctive/optative sense) 'you obtain/attain' takes رهایی 'release/freedom' as its object; ز من 'from me' is the source. The whole means 'If you want to attain release from me...'.
segments.s148-g20: سرافراز باشی is an exhortation/subjunctive meaning 'be exalted / be honored'; باشی here is the subjunctive/imperative form of 'to be'. به هر انجمن is a locative/occasional phrase 'in/at every assembly/gathering' specifying where one should be honored. The clause completes the conditional: '...be exalted in every assembly.'
segments.s148-g21: از ایران is a topical/oblique phrase 'concerning/from Iran' that sets the domain. هر آنچت 'whatever is yours' is the direct object; بپرسم is a first‑person verbal form 'I ask' (used here to introduce 'whenever I ask'), and بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive 'say/tell (it)'. The sequence is elliptical: 'Regarding Iran, whatever I ask of you, say it.'
segments.s148-g22: This segment repeats the admonition form seen earlier: متاب 'do not turn/waver', از ره راستی 'from the path of truth/honesty', هیچ روی 'do not in any way turn'. It is an emphatic prohibition: 'do not depart from the path of truth at all.'
segments.s148-g23: سپارم is a first‑person verbal form 'I entrust / I will entrust' and takes به تو as the indirect object 'to you'. گنج آراسته is the direct object 'an adorned/ornamented treasure' (noun + adjective following it). Word order is verb – indirect object – direct object, a common classical Persian order.
segments.s148-g24: بیابی is a second‑person/future or optative verbal form 'you will find / you may find'; بسی is an adverb 'much/many' or 'ample', qualifying خلعت و خواسته 'robes of honor and (other) rewards/desired things'. The clause promises the addressee plentiful gifts: 'you will find abundant robes and desires/rewards.'
segments.s148-g33: همه (quantifier) precedes the noun نامداران to mean “all/the whole of the famed ones.” نامداران (an adjective-turned-noun) + آن مرز functions as “the famed men of that border/region” — the classical Persian often omits the ezāfe, so آن مرز follows the noun it qualifies. را at the end is the definite/object marker (here marking the whole group being enumerated).
segments.s148-g34: چو here means “like/such as,” introducing examples; the poet repeats چو before names for rhetorical emphasis. The names are coordinated with و (“and”). The final را again marks the definite group being listed (an emphatic/object marker after the enumerated items).
segments.s148-g35: ز is the poetic variant of از (“from/about”) and begins a prepositional phrase: ز بهرام = “about Bahram.” The parallel construction ز ... و از ... places Bahram and رستم in the same frame; نامدار follows رستم as a postposed adjective (“the renowned Rostam”), a common classical/poetic ordering without an explicit ezāfe.
segments.s148-g36: ز هر کت = “from/out of every coat” (literally), where هر is the distributive “every” and کت means “coat/garment” (likely metonymic for each fighter/holder). بپرسم is a first-person present-subjunctive form used here with a future/intent sense “I will ask.” به من is the beneficiary “to me/for me,” and برشمار (base form without overt person marking) functions as an instruction or jussive “enumerate/count (for me).”
segments.s148-g37: بگو is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'say' introducing reported material. کان is an archaic particle introducing the content of the report (here functioning like English 'that' or 'that it was'); سراپرده is a compound noun 'inner‑curtain / inner pavilion' and هفت رنگ is a post‑nominal descriptive phrase 'seven‑colored' (هفت modifies رنگ, together forming the adjective 'seven‑colored' that qualifies سراپرده).
segments.s148-g38: بدو is the prepositional combination (به + او) 'to it / unto it' (here locative: 'in/within it'); اندرون is the spatial noun 'inside/inner quarters'; خیمه‌های is the plural 'tents' (the poetic line omits an explicit ezāfe or copula), and پلنگ is literal 'leopard' used to qualify the tents (i.e. 'leopard‑skin tents' or 'tents of the leopard').
segments.s148-g39: به پیش together functions as a locative prepositional phrase 'before / in front of'; اندرون names the inner chamber/quarters. بسته is a participial/adjectival form 'tied up / fastened' describing the following noun phrase, and صد ژنده‌پیل is the numeric noun phrase 'a hundred (صد) ragged/shaggy elephants' (classical Persian often omits an explicit verb, so 'stood/were' is understood).
segments.s148-g40: یکی is an itemizing 'one / a certain one' introducing a single specimen; مهد پیروزه is a noun ('cradle') modified by the adjective/ornament 'turquoise' (پیروزه), and برسان is the verbal form 'bringing / bearing' (here participial/attributive: 'one bearing...'). نیل is a color/stone word 'Nile‑blue / indigo' describing the hue of the cradle or its cloth. The copula is again omitted, so English supplies 'carried/was bearing'.
segments.s148-g57: دگر (degar) is an adverb/pronoun here meaning “another” or “one other”; گفت is the past-tense verb “said” (classical past 3rd person) introducing reported speech; کان is a demonstrative/particle here meaning “that” (archaic usage). پرده‌سرای is a compound noun (پرده + سرا) meaning a curtained hall or pavilion; سرخ is the adjective “red” placed before the noun, as is normal in Persian poetic word order.
segments.s148-g58: سواران is the plural noun “horsemen/riders” (subject of the clause); بسی گردش is a noun phrase: بسی here intensifies (many/ample) and گردش is “circuits/turns” (i.e. they made many circuits or paraded); اندر به پای is a prepositional phrase meaning “at (or around) the foot/feet of” — اندر + به + پای stacks classical prepositions for a locative sense (poetic register).
segments.s148-g59: یکی is the indefinite numeral/particle “one” or “a certain”; شیر پیکر is a compound adjective+noun meaning “lion-formed” or “lion-bodied” (شیر modifying پیکر); درفشی here denotes the banner/standard (poetic noun); به زر is an agentive/instrumental phrase meaning “with gold” or “gilded in gold” (به + material). Word order places the descriptive compounds before or adjacent to the head noun in typical poetic compression.
segments.s148-g60: درفشان is an adjective/participle meaning “shining” or “glittering” describing the next item; یکی repeats “one”/“another”; در میانش is the prepositional phrase “in its midst” (در = in, میانش = its middle); گهر is the noun “jewel/gem.” The clause lists a second banner (or object) and then specifies that it bore a jewel at its center.
segments.s148-g61: چنین گفت is a fixed formula “thus spoke / thus said” (چنین = thus, گفت = said); کان again is a demonstrative/particle introducing what follows (archaic “that”); فر آزادگان is a construct meaning “the farr (divine/royal glory) of the nobles/the free” (فر = divine glory/splendor, آزادگان = the nobles/free ones). The phrase is a short declarative naming a kind of splendour associated with the noble class.
segments.s148-g62: This segment lists names/titles. جهانگیر is used as a proper name or title (here a name), گودرز is the personal name Goudarz, and کشوادگان appears as a family-name or epithet; in this poetic list the names stand apposed to the preceding phrase (they are examples or bearers of that “فر”).
segments.s148-g63: بپرسید is a past-tense verb form meaning “asked” (classical/poetic past; the prefix ب- appears in older usage); کان again functions as the demonstrative/particle “that”; سبز پرده‌سرای is the noun phrase “the green pavilion/curtained hall,” with the adjective سبز placed before the noun in the usual Persian order.
segments.s148-g64: یکی لشکری is “a certain troop” or “one troop” (یکی + لشکری); گشن is an adjective “hungry” modifying لشکری; پیشش is a locative adverb/pronoun meaning “before it / in its presence” (پیش + possessive -ش); به پای is the locative phrase “at (its) feet” (به + پای). Together the clause says a hungry body of troops stood before the pavilion at its foot.
segments.s148-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. افگند is a past tense verb (poetic form of افکند) 'cast/threw' and here precedes its subject (verb–subject order, common in classical Persian). خور is the subject 'the sun'; سوی بالا is an adverbial phrase 'toward up/high' modifying افگند; کمند is the direct object 'lasso/snare' that the sun cast.
segments.s148-g2: زبانه is the nominal subject 'a tongue/flare (of flame)'. برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'from the high sky/wheel' (چرخ = sky; بلند postmodifies چرخ). The clause is a simple subject–verb with a source phrase.
segments.s148-g3: بپوشید is a past transitive verb 'he put on/was clothed in' and here appears before the proper name (verb–subject order). سهراب is the subject 'Sohrab'. خفتان functions here as a nominal/participial element linked to جنگ (i.e. the thing put on is described with جنگ, forming خفتانِ جنگ as the object of بپوشید); the given sense list only provides the lemma 'to sleep' but grammatically it is used as a noun/participle in the object slot.
segments.s148-g4: نشست is the past intransitive 'he sat/settled'. از بر is a classical locative/ablative phrase; here it yields the sense 'upon/on' (he sat upon). چرمه is the noun 'leather/hide' and سنگ رنگ is a compound-like postmodification meaning 'stone‑colored' (سنگ modifies رنگ or forms a compound adjective), so چرمه سنگ رنگ is the noun phrase indicating what he sat on.
segments.s148-g5: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one/a certain' placed before the noun. تیغ هندی is the noun phrase 'Indian sword' (noun + adjective). به چنگ is an idiomatic instrumental/locative phrase 'in the grip/in the hand' and اندرش is the classical prepositional + enclitic pronoun (اندر + ش) meaning 'in it/his (hand)' — together giving 'in his hand'.
segments.s148-g6: یکی again 'one/a certain'. مغفر here means a 'covering/veil/head‑covering' (not the abstract 'forgiveness') and خسروی is a postpositive adjective 'royal/kingly' modifying مغفر. بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'on his head' with the possessive enclitic ش attached to سر.
segments.s148-g7: کمندی is the noun 'a lasso/lanyard'. به فتراک is a genitive/attachment phrase 'to the saddle‑cloth' (به indicating attachment). بر شست is a locative 'on (the) thumb' (بر + noun) with شست = 'thumb'. خم is an adjectival/participial description 'bent' describing the lanyard.
segments.s148-g8: خم اندر خم is a poetic reduplication meaning literally 'bend within bend' (خم = 'bend/curve'; اندر = 'in/within'); و links the next phrase. روی کرده دژم is a participial construction: روی 'face', کرده (past participle of کردن) 'made/turned', and دژم the adjective 'sullen/grim' — together 'with his face made sullen' or 'his face turned grim'.
segments.s148-g89: نشان داده بود is a past perfect construction: نشان (a sign/token) is the patient, داده بود = "had been given/shown." The prepositional phrase از پدر مادرش follows the verb and marks the source/agents (from his father and mother), with -ش as the possessive clitic "his."
segments.s148-g90: همی دید is an iterative/persistent past: همی + past-verb = "kept looking/was continually seeing." و links two clauses. دیده here = "the sight / what the eyes saw," and نبد (an archaic negative copula) = "was not / did not become;" باورش = "his belief." Together: he repeatedly looked, but what his eyes showed did not become (or win) his belief.
segments.s148-g91: همی here gives an ongoing/iterative sense: "continually/kept." نام جست uses جست (literally "leapt/sprang") in a poetic sense: "the name sprang from / was uttered from"; از زبان هجیر marks the source of utterance (from Hejir's tongue / by Hejir's speech).
segments.s148-g92: مگر کان here has a concessive/optative shade: "as if / perhaps." سخنها (plural) = "those words/speeches." شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن "become" (here: 'might become / should be'), and دلپذیر = "pleasing/pleasant to the heart." The clause expresses hope or wish that the uttered words might please the heart.
segments.s148-g93: نبشته (an inscription / written text) is the subject; به سر بر = "upon the head" (به + سر + بر/بر here marks location; poetic word order places location after the noun). دگرگونه بود = "was different/other." The sequence says that the writing placed on (or at) the head was of a different kind.
segments.s148-g94: ز فرمان = "from the command/decree" (ز = from). نکاهد is a negative present form meaning "does not lessen / will not take away (from)." نخواهد فزود = "he will not add/increase" (نخواهد + فزود, future/modal construction). Together they state he neither subtracts from nor adds to the order.
segments.s148-g95: ازان پس = "after that / thereafter." بپرسید is a past-narrative form "they asked" in older/persian narrative. زان مهتران = "of those elders/chiefs" (زان = 'from those / of those'), so the clause means they inquired of those elders.
segments.s148-g96: کشیده is a participle/adjective (drawn/extended). سراپرده = "the inner curtain / inner chamber curtain." بد = "badly / ill / poorly." برکران (poetic compound) = "at the edge / on the threshold/border." The sequence describes that the inner curtain had been drawn/placed badly at the edge (i.e., improperly arranged).
segments.s148-g121: به گرد اندرش is a prepositional phrase meaning “around it / around its body”: به + گرد (here ‘around’) + اندر + ش (enclitic = ‘its’). The three color adjectives سرخ و زرد و بنفش follow in sequence and enumerate the colours of the thing being described.
segments.s148-g122: ز هرگونه‌ای برکشیده درفش is a nominal phrase describing the banner: ز (poetic ‘of/from’ used here roughly as ‘of’ or ‘in’) + هرگونه‌ای (‘of every kind’) modifies برکشیده درفش (‘a banner raised’). برکشیده is a participial/adjectival past form ‘raised’, and درفش is the head noun ‘banner/standard’. The whole gives ‘a banner raised of every kind’ (i.e. in every hue).
segments.s148-g123: درفشی پس پشت پیکرگراز: درفشی (here functioning as ‘a banner/standard’) + پس پشت (‘behind / at the rear of’) + پیکرگراز (‘the boar-bodied figure’, a compound noun). Word order places the banner first, then gives its position relative to the boar-figure.
segments.s148-g124: سرش ماه زرین و بالا دراز: سر + ش = ‘its head’; ماه زرین = ‘a golden moon’ (ماه used as a bright, round ornament); و links the next attribute; بالا دراز = ‘high and long’ (بالا here ‘high/lofty’, دراز ‘long’), describing the banner’s upper shaft/pole. The clauses are adjectival appositions describing parts of the banner.
segments.s148-g125: چنین گفت کاو را گرازست نام is an act-of-speech formula plus naming: چنین گفت = ‘thus said’; کاو را = object/indirect object (kâv here functions as an old pronoun or deictic; کاو را = ‘to him / he’ in older usage); گرازست نام = ‘(its) name is Gharâz’ (گرازست is a nominal/copular formation meaning ‘it is Gharâز / boar-like’ and نام = ‘name’). The sequence reflects poetic, somewhat archaic word order.
segments.s148-g126: که در جنگ شیران ندارد لگام is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by که (‘for/that’): در جنگ شیران = ‘in battle with/against lions’; ندارد لگام = ‘does not have a bridle/restraint’ (ندارد = ‘does not have’, لگام = ‘bridle/restraint’). Together: ‘for in battle against lions it has no bridle’ (i.e. is uncontrollable/brave).
segments.s148-g127: هشیوار و ز تخمه گیوگان: هشیوار = ‘alert/aware’ (predicate or attributive adjective); و = ‘and’; ز تخمه = ‘of the seed/stock (from the line of)’ where تخمه means ‘offspring/lineage’ here; گیوگان = ‘the Givogān’ (a tribal/familial designation). Together they describe origin and quality: ‘alert and from the lineage of the Givogān.’
segments.s148-g128: که بر درد و سختی نگردد ژگان is a relative clause modifying the stock: که = ‘who/that’; بر درد و سختی = ‘in the face of pain and hardship’ (بر here = ‘upon / in the presence of’); نگردد = negative subjunctive/optative of گشت/گردیدن ‘does not become/turn (does not turn into)’; ژگان is a name/collective (here functioning as the predicate of the negative: ‘do not become [called] Žagān’ or more idiomatically ‘do not give way / do not falter’). The clause means that members of this stock do not succumb under suffering.
segments.s148-g41: This noun phrase names the banner: یکی ‘a certain/one’ introduces it, برز is an adjective ‘lofty/exalted’ modifying the head noun درفش ‘banner’. خورشید پیکر is an attributive compound (literally “sun‑figure”) meaning “bearing the image/figure of the sun”; the genitive/attributive relation is implicit and expressed by juxtaposition in this poetic word order.
segments.s148-g42: سرش is سر + possessive suffix (its head/top). ماه زرین is a noun + adjective (‘a golden moon’), describing that finial; غلافش is غلاف + possessive (‘its sheath’). Because Persian often omits the copula, بنفش (‘purple’) is a predicate adjective: “its sheath (was) purple.”
segments.s148-g43: به + قلب سپاه = ‘in/at the heart of the army’; اندرون (‘within/inside’) further specifies location; جای (‘place/position’) is the noun being questioned. کی + ست form the interrogative ‘who is?’ (کی “who” + ست copula). The whole clause asks who occupies the army’s central position.
segments.s148-g44: ز here means ‘from/of’ (poetic) and introduces the source group (گردان ایران = ‘the regiment/division of Iran’). ورا is the clitic ‘to/for him’ used with the following predicate. نام چیست is ‘what is (the) name?’ (نام ‘name’ + چی ‘what’ + ست copula): literally, ‘from the Iranian troop, to him, what is the name?’ — i.e. “what is his name among the Iranian troops?”
segments.s148-g45: بدو = ‘to him’. گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’ (here introducing reported speech). کان is an archaic/poetic particle introducing the content of the report (‘that’); شاه ایران بود is the reported clause (‘he was the king of Iran’) with بود marking past tense. The whole segment = “he said to him that he was king of Iran.”
segments.s148-g46: بدرگاه او = ‘in his presence/at his court’ (بدرگاه as locative), where او = ‘he/his’. پیل و شیران بود is a nominal clause with بود (‘there were/was’) as copula: ‘elephants and lions were (present).’ The verb is explicit here.
segments.s148-g47: وزان پس = ‘and from that / thereafter’ (compound temporal connector), پس reinforcing ‘then’. بدو گفت = ‘he said to him’ (past). بر میمنه = بر + میمنه, a locative prepositional phrase ‘on the right (side)’ indicating where/which flank is being described.
segments.s148-g48: This is a coordinated noun list giving the forces: سواران بسیار = ‘many horsemen’ (سواران noun + بسیار ‘many’). The following و پیل و بنه continue the list: ‘and elephants and benē.’ The copula from the previous clause is understood (the existence/arrangement is asserted by the larger sentence).
segments.s148-g9: بیامد is a simple past verb 'came' placed before its subject (poetic inversion). یکی is the indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'. برز بالا is a noun + modifier meaning 'a lofty height / high place' (برز 'high' with بالا reinforcing 'up/high'). گزید is the past of گزیدن here meaning 'took/selected/occupied' — i.e. he took up or went to that elevated spot.
segments.s148-g10: به جایی introduces motion 'to a place' (به + جایی). که begins a relative clause 'where/that' modifying جایی. ایرانیان را is the definite object (را marks the object) 'the Iranians', and بدید is the past 'he saw' referring back to the same subject 'someone' from the previous clause.
segments.s148-g11: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said'. تا here connects the clauses causally ('so/and then/so that'), leading to رفت 'went' (simple past). پیشش 'before him / in his presence' indicates the direction/location of رفت; هجیر is the name of the person who went.
segments.s148-g12: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'); گفت is the reporting verb (the dataset lacks the verbal sense, so sense index is unavailable). The reported clause کژی نیاید ز تیر is a predication: کژی 'crookedness/error', نیاید a negative-present/optative-form 'does not come / will not occur', ز = 'from', تیر = 'arrow' — together, 'no crookedness will come from the arrow' (i.e. the arrow will not err).
segments.s148-g13: نشانه 'the mark/aim' is the topic. نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not / should not'. که introduces the clause describing what must not happen: خم آورد 'to bring/bear a bend' — here آورد means 'to cause/bring about' and خم = 'a bend/curve', so the line warns against the aim or shot becoming curved.
segments.s148-g14: چو = 'when'. پیچان (twisting) + شود ('becomes') forms a dependent clause 'when it becomes twisted'. زخم 'wound' is the subject of کم آورد, where کم 'little/insufficient' and آورد 'brings/causes' together mean 'produces little (damage)' — i.e. a twisted shot yields a slight wound.
segments.s148-g15: به هر کار 'in every task' (به + هر + کار). در پیشه 'in your craft/profession' is a locative phrase; کن is the imperative 'do', and راستی is the adverb/noun 'honesty/truthfulness' used adverbially — together: 'In every task of your craft, act honestly.'
segments.s148-g16: چو here means 'if' (conditional). خواهی is the second-person present 'you want/wish'. که introduces the content of that wish: نگزایدت is a negative present/subjunctive-form 'may not befall you / not harm you' with enclitic ت 'you' as the experiencer. کاستی 'lack/deficiency' is the thing you do not want to befall you — 'if you wish that deficiency not befall you...'.
segments.s148-g81: چنین گفت is an adverb + past verb: 'چنین' (thus/so) precedes the past verb 'گفت' (said). کز is the poetic contracted form of که + ز (or که + از) introducing a subordinate clause meaning 'that/who/from which.' چین here is the origin 'China.' یکی نامدار is a noun phrase where یکی = 'a certain/one' modifies the following adjective/epithet نامدار = 'renowned.'
segments.s148-g82: بنوی is treated here as a personal name (not the imperatival root 'write'), followed by the past verb بیامد = 'came.' In classical/poetic Persian بر is often used where Modern Persian uses به and here means 'to/toward' plus شهریار 'the king.' Word order is Subject (name) + Verb + prepositional phrase marking the goal.
segments.s148-g83: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'asked' (third-person). نامش 'his name' is the noun نام with the enclitic possessive ش. ز before a proper name is the poetic preposition 'from' (= از), so ز فرخ هجیر means 'from Farrokh Hejir' (i.e. they asked that person). Word order is Verb + object + prepositional phrase indicating the person asked.
segments.s148-g84: بدو is the clitic prepositional phrase 'to him/to them' (به + او). گفت is the past 'said' (here glossed as speech). نامش ندارم is elliptical: ندارم literally 'I do not have' used here to mean 'I do not know/have (the name).' بویر is given as a word (here functioning like a name or identifier). The line therefore reports Farrokh Hejir's reply.
segments.s148-g85: بدین دژ بدم من بدان روزگار is a temporal/location clause: بدین (به + این) 'to/at this', دژ 'fortress', followed by what appears as a first-person copular/predicate (بدم/بودم) and the pronoun من 'I', then بدان روزگار 'in that time/those days.' In poetry such forms and contractions (e.g. به→ب) are common; the clause means 'I was in this fortress in that time.'
segments.s148-g86: کجا is the interrogative 'where.' او is the third-person pronoun 'he.' بیامد is the simple past 'came.' بر شهریار uses the preposition بر = 'to/toward' + شهریار 'the king.' The clause asks 'Where did he come to the king?' with SOV/verb-final order typical of Persian clauses.
segments.s148-g87: غمی گشت is the verbless/periphrastic expression 'a sorrow became' = 'sorrow arose.' سهراب را دل places S ohrab as possessor/topic with را and then دل 'heart' (literally 'Sohrab's heart'); ازان 'from that' gives the cause. Word order reflects topicalization: 'Sorrow arose in Sohrab's heart because of that.'
segments.s148-g88: که introduces the content clause 'that ...' explaining the cause. جایی ز رستم نیامد نشان is literally 'no sign/trace came from Rostam anywhere': جایی here means 'anywhere/any place', ز = 'from', رستم = 'Rostam', نیامد = past negative 'did not come', and نشان = 'sign/trace.' Together they explain the reason for Sohrab's grief.
segments.s148-g25: ور is the conditional 'if' introducing a hypothetical. ایدون is a temporal/ablative verbal-adverbial ('now, when it comes/appears') that frames the condition. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...', کژی is an abstract noun 'wrongness/crookedness' functioning as the predicate, بود is the past copula 'was/were' marking the state, رای تو = 'your counsel/opinion' (رای is a noun followed by the possessor تو). Together: 'If now it came that your counsel was crooked...'.
segments.s148-g26: همان 'that same' points back to the consequence; بند و زندان is a noun pair joined by و meaning 'fetter and prison' (metonymic for bondage/punishment). بود is again the past copula 'was/would be' and جای تو = 'your place/lot' (جای + possessive). The sequence gives the result of the conditional: 'that same fetter and prison would be your place.'
segments.s148-g27: هجیرش is the proper name هجیر with the enclitic -ش (to him / for him) and here functions as the indirect object ('to/for Hejir' or 'Hejir received'); چنین 'thus' modifies داد 'gave' (past tense) and پاسخ 'answer' is the direct object. که introduces the reported speech/that-clause beginning with شاه 'king' as the vocative or addressee.
segments.s148-g28: This is the content of the answer. سخن 'word/thing' + هرچه 'whatever' (a free relative) is the object of پرسد — here پرسد is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person form used in reported speech ('you may ask' / 'you ask'); ز marks the domain 'about' or 'concerning', ایران سپاه = 'the army/host of Iran'. The whole phrase: 'whatever you ask concerning the Iranian army.'
segments.s148-g29: بگویم is a 1st-person jussive/subjunctive often used to express 'I will/let me say' or 'I will tell'. همه آنچ دانم = 'all that I know' where آنچ is a relative 'that which' and دانم is present 'I know'. بدوی here functions as a prepositional/enclitic expression meaning 'to you' (classical proclitic form), so the clause reads 'I will tell you all that I know.'
segments.s148-g30: به + کژی = 'about/with regard to wrongness/crookedness'; چرا 'why' is the interrogative particle, بایدم is the modal 'I must / should' (باید with enclitic -م for first person), and گفت‌وگوی (گفت‌وگو) 'discussion/debate' is the object. Together: 'Why should I have to debate about wrongness?'
segments.s148-g31: بدو is 'to him' (prepositional phrase or dative clitic 'to/for him'); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing reported content. کز (که + ز fused) functions as 'that / from which' introducing the subordinate clause; تو is the internal object 'you', and بپرسم is a first-person subjunctive/future-like form 'I will ask' (بپرسم literally 'that I ask/should ask') with همه 'all/everything' as its object: 'He said to him that he would ask you everything.'
segments.s148-g32: A simple coordinated list introduced by ز 'about/concerning': گردنکشان 'the rebels' (plural), و 'and', ز شاه 'about the king', و 'and', رمه 'the host/flock/army' — here رمه means the mass of troops or multitude. The sequence names the topics he intends to question about.
segments.s148-g113: ز is the preposition 'from' introducing the source (here the palanquin). هودج 'palanquin' is the noun governed by ز. فرو هشته is a compound: فرو 'down' + هشته 'tuft/lock', together 'a tuft hanging down'. دیبا جلیل is a noun + adjective ('brocade' qualified by 'majestic'), placed after the tuft it describes.
segments.s148-g114: غلام is the noun 'slave' and ایستاده is a participial adjective 'standing' (literally 'standing slaves'). رده 'row/rank' introduces the arrangement; the repeated خیل خیل is a poetic pluralizing/collective 'host upon host / crowd upon crowd', intensifying the multitude.
segments.s148-g115: بر is a locative preposition here meaning 'at' or 'by' (here 'by/at the tent'). خیمه 'tent' is followed by نزدیک + پرده‌سرای, where نزدیک 'near' qualifies the following noun پرده‌سرای ('the curtain‑hall' or stage area). The whole phrase = 'at the tent, near the curtain‑hall.'
segments.s148-g116: به دهلیز here introduces the location 'into/at the corridor' with به as directional/locative. چندی 'several' modifies پیاده 'on foot' or 'foot-soldiers' (i.e. some made up of footmen). The final به پای is an adverbial/locative phrase literally 'at the foot' or 'on foot/at attention' — به in this position functions adverbially and پای is 'foot'.
segments.s148-g117: بدو is the clitic pronoun 'to him' (lit. 'unto him'), and گفت is the verb 'said' (past). کاو را is a noun phrase with را marking the direct object (here 'that one' or 'him/them'); فریبرز is the personal name that follows را as the object of خوان. خوان here is the verb 'call/name' (an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'name him / call him').
segments.s148-g118: که introduces an explanatory clause 'because/that'. فرزند is 'son/child' and شاهست is an archaic copular construction with شاه (king) — the clause means 'he is a son of the king' (literally 'son [of] king-is'). و is the conjunction 'and', تاج 'crown', and گوان is a qualifier meaning 'like/worthy of' or 'such as' (poetic), so تاج گوان = 'worthy/fit for the crown.'
segments.s148-g119: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'he asked/inquired'. کان here is an archaic demonstrative 'that' (not one of the listed senses), and سرخ 'red' modifies it; پرده‌سرای 'curtain‑hall / stage‑room' completes the noun phrase: 'that red one of the curtain‑hall' (i.e. 'who is that red‑clad person in the curtain‑hall?').
segments.s148-g120: This repeats the locational/positional phrase from earlier: به دهلیز 'in/into the corridor', چندی 'several', پیاده 'on foot / footmen', and به پای as an adverbial 'at the foot/at attention'. The verb is omitted here (typical poetic ellipsis) and the phrase describes the scene in the corridor.
segments.s148-g65: This is a nominal phrase describing location and quality. یکی here means 'a certain' or 'one' (indefinite article sense), تخت is 'throne' (the object), پرمایه is an adjectival attribute meaning 'rich/abundant' or 'well‑appointed', and اندر میان gives the locative 'in/within the middle' (اندر = in, میان = middle). Word order is adjective after noun (تخت پرمایه) and then the locative phrase.
segments.s148-g66: This clause is verbal/adjectival in sense: زده here functions as a past participle/adjectival verb meaning 'struck' or 'set/appeared' (poetic use often conveys 'shone' or 'was set'), پیش او = 'before it / in front of it' (پیش = before/in presence of, او = it/him referring back to the throne), and اختر کاویان is a compound noun 'the Kayanian star' (i.e. a notable Kayanian figure likened to a star). The sequence places the participle before its locative complement and the noun phrase that names what stood/appeared.
segments.s148-g67: برو بر نشسته is a compact descriptive phrase: نشسته is a past participle 'seated' (acting as predicate), and برو بر is an adverbial/locative cluster meaning 'up on / atop' (بر = on/upon; برو here has an adverbial sense 'up/aloft' in classical/poetic usage). یکی پهلوان is the indefinite subject 'a certain champion'. The order places the locative/adverbial before the participle and then the noun phrase identifying who is seated.
segments.s148-g68: This is a coordinated string of attributes introduced by the preposition با 'with'. ابا فر appears as an epithet containing فر ('farr', royal radiance); ابا here is archaic/integrated into the epithet and functions together with فر as an attributive phrase. Then و = 'and', با سفت = 'with (firm/tightly bound) (سفت)' and و یال گوان = 'and a mane (یال) like (گوان) [something]'. Grammatically it is a list of qualities attached to the preceding subject (the champion).
segments.s148-g69: This relative clause begins with the poetic preposition ز = 'from/of'. هر کس 'every person' is followed by the relative که 'who'. بر پای پیشش براست is the predicate: بر پای = 'on (his) feet' (بر = on, پای = foot/stance), پیشش = 'in his presence / before him', and براست is an adjectival/verb form meaning 'is upright / stands erect'. The clause therefore means 'from every person who stands upright before him.'
segments.s148-g70: نشسته is the participle 'seated'. به یک رش is a measure phrase 'by one small amount / by a little' (به = by/with, یک = one, رش = a small measure/quantity in this idiom). سرش = 'his head' (سر + possessive ش), برتر است = 'is higher / is superior' (comparative predicate). The clause literally says 'seated, his head is higher by a little.'
segments.s148-g71: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase 'once / suddenly' (یکی = one, باره = occasion/instance, together often 'at one time' or 'suddenly'). پیشش = 'before him / in his presence' and به بالای اوی = 'to/over his head' (به بالای = up above, اوی = his). The sequence places temporal adverbial (suddenness) before the locative phrase that situates the event relative to the person.
segments.s148-g72: کمندی is the noun 'a lasso / snare' (subject). فرو هشته is a compound verbal/participial description: فرو 'down' (directional adverb) plus هشته (a tuft/lock — here describing a hanging lock or cluster), together conveying 'hung down with a lock/tuft'. تا introduces the extent 'as far as', پای اوی = 'his feet' (پای = foot, اوی = his). The clause therefore says the lasso hung down so that its lock reached to his feet.
segments.s148-g137: ازان (aẕān) is a prepositional source phrase 'from that / from it' introducing what issues forth; پرده سبز is a noun (پرده) followed by its adjective (سبز) — classical Persian normally places the adjective after the noun. مرد بلند is a noun + adjective phrase ('the tall man'); no finite verb is expressed here (a common poetic ellipsis: 'came/was seen').
segments.s148-g138: وزان is a compound of و + زان meaning 'and from that / and from it', linking a second list of things coming from the same source. اسپ 'steed/horse' is the head noun; تاب‌داده is a past-participle (تاب + داده) meaning 'having been swung/whirled' and modifies کمند 'lasso/snare' (i.e. 'that lasso which has been swung').
segments.s148-g139: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that'; هجیر is the personal name (subject) and سپهبدش is the title سپهبد with the third-person possessive enclitic -ش ('his spahbed')—an appositive indicating Hejir's rank. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' concluding the main clause.
segments.s148-g140: که introduces the subordinate/interrogative content clause ('that/which' → here: 'what...'); از تو is a prepositional phrase 'from you' (marking the person from whom something would be hidden); سخن را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker attached to سخن 'word/matter'; چه باید نهفت is an interrogative construction with چه 'what' + modal باید 'ought/must' + نهفت (a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'to hide / be hidden'), yielding 'what ought to be hidden'.
segments.s148-g141: گر is the conditional 'if'; از نام چینی is a noun phrase literally 'from/of the name Chini' used here as 'by the name "Chini" / called Chini' (classical idiom); بمانم is the present/subjunctive 1st‑person singular of ماندن 'to remain' (here 'I remain / I am called'), and همی is an archaic adverbial particle meaning 'still' or a habitual/continuous marker often used in epic verse.
segments.s148-g142: ازان است = 'from that it is' (i.e. 'that is why'); کاو here is an archaic fused form (ک + او) functioning as 'that one / him' (classical poetic pronoun) rather than the literal lexical sense 'to search'; را is the object/ topical marker attached to کاو; ندانم is the 1st‑person present singular negative of دانستن 'I do not know'; همی again is the habitual/continuative particle — together: 'that is why I do not know him.'
segments.s148-g143: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (ب + دو → 'unto him'); گفت is the past-tense 'said' (here introducing Sohrab's words); سهراب is the speaker's name; کاین is the demonstrative 'that/this' (ک + این), and نیست is the negation 'is not' — together starting the reported clause 'that is not so/that is not the case'. داد in this line functions idiomatically (classical usage) and does not denote a straightforward 'give' here; the exact idiomatic nuance is 'case/affair' or 'so' in context.
segments.s148-g144: ز here is the poetic preposition 'concerning / of' (introducing the topic 'Rostam'); رستم is the name Rostam; نکردی is the 2nd‑person singular past negative of کردن 'you did not do' (here 'you did not say / you did not speak'); سخن 'speech/word' is the object; هیچ یاد literally 'any remembrance' functions as an emphatic negation 'not a word / not at all' — thus 'you did not speak a word about Rostam.'
segments.s148-g185: بدو گفت is the archaic/poetic equivalent of به او گفت (“he said to him”); the sequence is object‑phrase + verb. سهراب is the speaker (proper name). از آزادگان is an appositive/genitive phrase meaning “from/of the free (nobles),” identifying Sohrab as belonging to that class.
segments.s148-g186: سیه بخت is a preposed adjectival epithet meaning “ill‑fated” (literally ‘black‑fortune’). گودرز is the proper name being qualified. کشوادگان is an epithet or family/tribal name attached to Goudarz; its exact historical reading is archaic/uncertain but functions like a surname or epithet here.
segments.s148-g187: چرا introduces an interrogative (“why”). چون here functions temporally/causally as “when” or “since” (idiomatically linking the clause that follows). ترا (clitic form of تو) is the object “you.” خواند is the classical/poetic past/subjunctive form of ‘call/summon’ (here “calls”). باید is the impersonal/modal verb “must/ought,” and پسر is the noun “son” (the logical subject of the obligation). Word order is somewhat archaising: the modal predicate باید follows the noun it scopes over.
segments.s148-g188: بدین is a fused prepositional phrase (به + این) meaning “by/with this” or “with such.” زور means physical or martial “strength/might.” و این دانش و این هنر lists two further nouns introduced by و and the demonstrative این: “this knowledge and this skill.” The phrase as a whole specifies the means or qualities with which the son is (supposed to be) associated.
segments.s148-g189: تو is the vocative/second‑person pronoun “you.” مردان جنگی is a compound noun phrase “warlike men / warriors” addressing the audience. کجا is the interrogative “where,” and دیده‌ای is the second‑person perfect/past form “have you seen” (classical word order places the interrogative before the verb).
segments.s148-g190: که introduces a relative/content clause (“that/which”). بانگ here means “cry/shout/sound” (the outcry or neigh). پی is a preposition/adverb meaning “after” or “following.” اسپ is the archaic word for “steed/horse.” نشنیده‌ای is the negative perfect/past second person “you have not heard” (ن‑ + شنیده‌ای). The clause modifies کجا دیده‌ای: “where have you seen that you have not heard the cry after a steed?”
segments.s148-g191: که again introduces the explanatory clause (“that/for”). چندین means “many times/often.” ز is the poetic variant of از meaning “of/about.” رستم is the proper name Rostam. سخن here functions as a verbal noun “speech/talk” and with بایدت (باید + enclitic ت “for you”) expresses obligation: “you must speak” or “it is necessary for you to speak.”
segments.s148-g192: زبان is “tongue” = “speech” (here the organ of speaking). بر is the preposition “upon/for.” ستودنش combines the verb ستودن “to praise” with the object clitic -ش “him” (i.e. “praising him”). بگشایدت is an optative/subjunctive form meaning “may (it) open for you” or “let (your tongue) open for you,” with the enclitic ت marking the beneficiary (‘for you’). The phrase is a wish or exhortation: “may your tongue open to praise him.”
segments.s148-g169: Literal word order groups noun + noun + adjective: 'سخن گفت ناگفته' functions as a nominal phrase 'the utterance/word that is unsaid'. 'چون' is the simile marker 'like', and 'گوهرست' is 'گوهر است' contracted (noun + copula) meaning 'is a gem'.
segments.s148-g170: 'کجا' introduces the locative/relative clause 'where', 'نابسوده' describes the state 'unspoiled/untouched', and 'به سنگ اندرست' is a locative clause with 'به' = into/in and 'اندرست' = 'is within' (compound of اندر + -است). Together: 'where it lies untouched within the stone.'
segments.s148-g171: 'چو' here = 'when'. 'از بند و پیوند' is an ablative phrase 'from fetter and bond', and 'یابد رها' literally 'finds (itself) free' — 'یابد' (present/epic tense 'finds/becomes') plus the predicate adjective 'رها' ('free/unbound'), so: 'when it becomes freed from bonds.'
segments.s148-g172: 'درخشنده' (shining/radiant) modifies 'مهری' (here a noun meaning a precious thing/jewel or token of affection). 'بود' is the copula ('was/was then' — epic past/presentive), and 'بی‌بها' is an adjective 'priceless'. The clause states the resulting state: 'it was a shining, priceless gem.'
segments.s148-g173: Word-for-word: 'چنین داد پاسخ هجیرش' = 'thus gave (he) the reply to Hejir'. 'داد' is past 'gave' with an omitted subject (typical narrative), and the enclitic '-ش' on 'هجیر' marks 'to him' (the object). 'که' introduces the content clause that follows about the king.
segments.s148-g174: 'چو' = 'when' (temporal). 'سیر آید از مهر' = 'becomes sated/filled from love' where 'سیر آید' is a light-verb expression 'comes to satiety'. 'وز' here is an archaic fused form (و + ز) meaning 'and from', so 'وز تاج و گاه' = 'and from crown and throne/place' — listing the other things with which the king might be sated. 'آید' is the present/habitual narrative 'comes'.
segments.s148-g175: The clause 'نبرد کسی جوید اندر جهان' is a general statement: 'no one in the world seeks battle' (literally 'seeks a fight'), with the word order placing the object 'نبرد' (battle) before the verb phrase. 'جوید اندر' is an archaic compounded verb 'seeks in/among' meaning 'seeks'.
segments.s148-g176: 'که' gives the reason: 'that/because he ...'. 'او' = 'he' (subject). 'ژنده پیل' is a noun phrase where 'ژنده' describes the elephant (here 'mangled/stripped/ragged' — a violently handled elephant), 'اندر آرد' = 'brings in/brings forth' (here: brings down), and 'ز جان' = 'from life' (poetic ablative) meaning 'takes away its life' (i.e., kills). The clause explains why no one seeks to fight him.
segments.s148-g105: 'بدو گفت' is a short phrase meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' + گفت = 'said'); the personal subject is omitted. 'زان سوی' is a prepositional phrase 'from that side' (زان = 'from that', سوی = 'side/direction'). 'تابنده شید' is a poetic noun phrase in which تابنده ('shining') modifies شید ('beloved' or the beloved figure), so the whole phrase describes a radiant/gleaming beloved figure on that side.
segments.s148-g106: برآید is a 3rd-person present (or present-subjunctive) verb meaning 'appears/rises'. یکی پرده is an indefinite noun phrase 'one/a certain curtain' (یکی = 'one/a certain', پرده = 'curtain/veil'). بینم is first-person present ('I see' or 'I behold') used by the speaker to report perception. سپید is an adjective 'white' describing the پرده.
segments.s148-g107: ز دیبای رومی uses ز ('of/from') to mark the material or characteristic — 'of Rûm brocade' — describing the rider. به پیشش is a locative/relational phrase 'before him/in his presence' (به = 'to/for', پیشش = 'before him'). سوار is the noun 'rider' (a mounted man) described by the preceding phrase.
segments.s148-g108: رده برکشیده is a compound descriptive expression: رده ('rank/row') + برکشیده ('raised/drawn up'), so 'ranks drawn up.' فزون از هزار is an idiomatic quantity phrase where فزون ('abundant/more') combines with از ('than/from') to mean 'more than a thousand.'
segments.s148-g109: پیاده سپردار is a noun phrase where پیاده ('infantry/on foot') qualifies سپردار ('commander'), so 'infantry commander' or 'foot commander.' The conjunction و links this to نیزه‌وران, a plural agent noun 'spearmen' or 'lancers.'
segments.s148-g110: شده is a past-participial/copular form meaning 'has become' or 'was made,' here introducing the result noun phrase 'انجمن لشکری بی‌کران' — 'an assembly/host of troops without end.' لشکری functions as 'troops/soldiery' and بی‌کران is an adjective 'boundless/countless.' The verbal predicate precedes the noun phrase, a common classical word order.
segments.s148-g111: نشسته is a participial/adjectival form 'seated' describing سپهدار ('the commander' or 'spahbed'). بر تخت عاج is a locative phrase 'on an ivory throne' (بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne', عاج = 'ivory'), with the material following the noun in classical ordering.
segments.s148-g112: نهاده is a perfective/participial verb 'having placed' or 'he had placed.' بران ('upon that') refers back to the previously mentioned ivory; عاج کرسی ساج is a noun phrase 'an ivory (piece) — a seat (کرسی) of saj (ساج) [wood]. Word order places the demonstrative/location word (بران) before the described object.
segments.s148-g97: This is a coordinated noun phrase: 'سواران بسیار و پیلان' = 'many horsemen and elephants.' 'سواران' is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders'; 'بسیار' here quantifies them ('many'). The coordinating conjunction 'و' links the two plural nouns. The following short phrase 'به پای' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'to (their) feet' or 'to stand up' (literally 'to the foot'), placed after the nouns as the scene-setting location/manner for the upcoming verb.
segments.s148-g98: 'برآید' is a third-person singular verbal form meaning 'rises/appears' and with the particle 'همی' (here used in the classical/epic sense) yields a habitual or continuous sense: 'keeps rising' or 'was continually rising.' 'ناله کرنای' is a possessive/genitive grouping 'the lament (ناله) of the horn/trumpet (کرنا)' — Persian commonly strings the two nouns without an overt ezāfe in poetic usage. Word order here is verb + adverbial particle + noun phrase, producing 'the trumpet's lament rose continuously.'
segments.s148-g99: 'یکی' is an indefinite demonstrative 'one' or 'a certain' introducing the item. 'گرگ پیکر' is a compound attributive phrase meaning 'wolf‑bodied' or 'shaped like a wolf' (گرگ = wolf, پیکر = form/body), and it modifies 'درفش' (standard/banner). The prepositional phrase 'از برش' indicates the source 'from his/its side' (classical poetic usage), i.e. where the banner was taken from.
segments.s148-g100: 'برآورده' is a past participle/adjectival form meaning 'raised/drawn forth' (the result of برآوردن). It introduces the resulting image: 'drawn forth.' The phrase 'از پرده زرین' is a source/instrument phrase 'from the golden curtain/veil' (پرده = curtain, زرین = golden). The enclitic 'ش' on 'سر' (سرش) is the possessive 'his/its' and yields 'its head' or 'his head'; here it refers back to the banner/figure, so 'its head emerged.' The whole segment describes that the head (of the wolf figure on the standard) appeared from the golden cloth.
segments.s148-g101: 'بدو گفت' is a compact clause 'he said to him' where 'بدو' is the dative pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb (classical order). 'کان' is an archaic/demonstrative introducer ('that' / 'that one'), and 'پور گودرز گیو' is a patronymic apposition meaning 'Giv, son of Goudarz' (پور = 'son of' as a name element). The segment functions to point out the person being named.
segments.s148-g102: The particle 'که' introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'whom/that.' 'خوانند' is a present plural verbal form used impersonally here: 'they call' (i.e., 'is called by people'). 'گردان' is the noun 'troop' (here likely part of a title or epithet), and 'ورا' functions as an old‑style pronominal/adverbial element 'to him/there' tied to the noun phrase. The final words 'گیو نیو' present the name/epithet by which he is known ('Giv Niu'); overall the clause means 'whom they call Giv Niu' or more loosely 'whom people call Giv Niu.'
segments.s148-g103: The preposition 'ز' here has the classical sense 'among' or 'of' (used poetically). 'گودرزیان' names the group 'the Goudarzis' (descendants/followers of Goudarz). 'مهتر و بهترست' is a coordinated predicate: 'مهتر' = 'greater/chief' and 'بهترست' = 'is better/best' — together 'he is foremost and best.' The sentence drops an explicit subject (common in Persian epic style) and presents the predicate after the genitive phrase.
segments.s148-g104: 'به ایرانیان' means 'for the Iranians' or 'to the Iranians' with به marking the beneficiary/standard. 'بر دو بهره' is a prepositional phrase literally 'over two shares' (بهره = 'share/turn/respect'), an idiom meaning 'in two respects' or 'in two ways.' 'سرست' is a predicative construction with 'سر' = 'head' plus the copula ending '-ست' (is), so 'سرست' = 'is head/leader.' The whole segment says he is head/leader for the Iranians in two respects.
segments.s148-g177: کسی را که ... هم‌نبرد is a nominal phrase with a restrictive relative clause. کسی ('someone') is marked by را (here the object/indefinite-marking use) and followed by که introducing the relative clause 'that/who'. رستم بود هم‌نبرد literally orders as 'Rostam was comrade' (بود is the past copula), so the clause means 'who was Rostam's comrade in battle' (هم‌نبرد is a compound noun 'comrade‑in‑arms').
segments.s148-g178: This clause states a dramatic consequence. سرش ('his head') + ز آسمان اندر آید ('comes down from the sky into') places the verb آید (3sg 'comes') at the end, as is normal in classical Persian. ز here is the poetic 'from', اندر means 'into/within', and به گرد literally 'to the dust' (به + گرد, with گرد used as 'dust') expresses motion toward the ground.
segments.s148-g179: تنش زور دارد is a possessive construction: تن + ـش ('his body') + زور دارد ('has strength'). به صد زورمند is a comparative/measure phrase: به here indicates 'equivalent to' or 'in the amount of', so the whole means 'his body has the strength of a hundred mighty ones.'
segments.s148-g180: سرش برترست از درخت بلند is a simple nominal clause with the predicate برترست ('is superior/is loftier') following the subject سرش ('his head'). از introduces the standard of comparison ('than'), so the line reads 'his head is higher than a tall tree.' برترست is an archaic/poetic present form meaning 'is higher/better.'
segments.s148-g181: چنو (archaic variant of چنان) means 'such' or 'so'; خشم گیرد uses گیرد (3sg present/subjunctive of گرفتن) to mean 'takes/grips (him)', so the phrase 'چنو خشم گیرد به‌روز نبرد' literally 'when such anger seizes (him) on the day of battle.' به‌روز here is a poetic time‑phrase 'on the day (of)'.
segments.s148-g182: The correlative چه ... و چه ... is used like 'whether ... or ...'. هم‌رزم او is 'his comrade'; ژنده پیل is a compound image 'ragged (ژنده) elephant (پیل)' (an epithet), and چه ... مرد completes 'whether ... or a man.' The clause sets up alternatives: 'whether his ally be a ragged elephant or a (mere) man.'
segments.s148-g183: هم‌آورد او is an attributive phrase: هم‌آورد ('equal, match') + او ('his'), meaning 'his equal'. بر زمین پیل نیست literally inverts the usual order: 'on earth (بر زمین) there is no elephant (پیل)'; with نیست asserting nonexistence, so the line says 'no elephant on earth matches him.'
segments.s148-g184: This is a simile introduced by چو ('as/like'). گرد here means 'dust' (the cloud raised by a horse), پی means 'after/following' (the trail of), and رخش او 'his steed, Rakhsh'. نیل نیست ('there is no Nile') uses the river Nile as a hyperbolic standard; taken together the clause says 'there is no Nile like the dust following his steed' — i.e. the dust behind his horse surpasses any river (poetic hyperbole).
segments.s148-g201: بگویم is a first‑person present/subjunctive/optative form of گفتن ('to say') often used as 'I will say' or 'let me say' in epic narration. بدین is the prepositional phrase به + این ('to/at this'), here indicating the addressee/target; ترک is the noun 'Turk' (the one addressed). با زور دست is an adverbial phrase 'by/with the force of (my) hand' where با + noun expresses instrument ('by/using') and the genitive relation is implicit (poetic word order).
segments.s148-g202: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; یال is the noun 'mane' (literally the hair of a horse or metaphorically a crest). و connects the two coordinated noun phrases; این خسروانی is 'this/this sort of royal/kingly (thing)', where خسروانی is an adjective or nominalized adjective meaning 'royal, kingly'. نشست here functions as a noun 'sitting/seat/session' (common in epic diction for a royal posture or seat), so the phrase describes the mane and the royal seat/air associated with him.
segments.s148-g203: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'with/from the side of'; لشکر 'host/army' is its complement. کند is the present tense third‑person singular of کردن 'to do/make' used here with جنگ ('to make war' = 'wage war'); او is the subject pronoun 'he'. The clause thus means 'he wages war with the army.'
segments.s148-g204: برانگیزد is a present/future‑oriented verb 'to arouse/provoke/raise' (third person subjunctive/optative form in epic diction). این باره is 'this matter/this affair' (این = 'this', باره = 'case/occasion'). پیلتن is a place name (complement specifying where or in which assembly he raises the matter). Together: 'he raises this affair at Piltan/among the Piltan assembly.'
segments.s148-g205: برین is the fused preposition بر + این (on/over/by this); بر here functions idiomatically as 'by/through' in a list of means. زور 'strength', و 'and', این کتف 'this shoulder' and و این یال اوی 'and this mane of his' form a coordinated list of the enemy's attributes. اوی is the possessive/personal form 'his' (here marking that the mane belongs to him).
segments.s148-g206: شود is the third‑person singular of شدن used as an auxiliary/passive marker here ('become / be (passive)' = 'will be'); کشته is the past‑participle 'killed/slain' (so شود کشته = 'will be killed'). رستم is the patient/name. به + چنگال indicates the instrument/place 'by/in the claw' where به functions as 'by/with' instrumentally; چنگال 'claw' is the instrument and اوی again is the possessive 'his' (the enemy's claw).
segments.s148-g207: از ایران نیاید is a negative optative/prohibitive construction: از ایران 'from Iran'; نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive of آمدن meaning here 'should not come' or 'let there not come'. کسی is 'anyone/no one' (indefinite), and کینه خواه is a compound meaning 'one who desires vengeance' (کینه 'grudge/hatred' + خواه 'desiring' used adjectivally). The whole clause expresses a prohibition: 'No one from Iran who desires vengeance should come.'
segments.s148-g208: بگیرد is the subjunctive/optative of گرفتن 'to seize/take' (here purposive: 'to take/seize'). سر تخت is a genitival phrase 'the head of the throne' or idiomatically 'the top/leadership of the throne' (سر = 'head/top', تخت = 'throne'). کاووس شاه names the king (Kavus) with شاه as apposition/title. Grammatically the clause states the forbidden purpose: 'to seize the head/top of King Kavus's throne.'
segments.s148-g145: This is a relative/nominal phrase: 'کسی' (someone/anyone) + the relative particle expressed here as the fused form 'کاو' (classical poetic contraction of 'که او', 'who') + the past copula 'بود' (was) + the predicate 'پهلوان جهان' (champion of the world). Word order is topical: the indefinite 'کسی' starts the clause and the identifying predicate follows. 'کاو' functions to link 'کسی' to the verb 'بود'.
segments.s148-g146: This locative phrase says where such a person would not be: 'میان' (in the middle/among) + 'سپه' (the army/host) + the negative/past-form 'نماند' (did not remain) + 'نهان' (hidden). Classical Persian often places the locative before the verb; the verb 'نماند' negates the possibility of remaining hidden.
segments.s148-g147: 'تو' (you) is the subject; 'گفتی' here is the past verb 'you said' (classical past simple). 'که' introduces the reported content ('that'), then 'بر لشکر او مهترست' is a predicative clause: 'بر' (over) marks the relation to 'لشکر' (army), 'او' (his) is the possessor, and 'مهترست' (is chief) is the predicate (copular adjective/title). Word order places the report verb before the reported clause.
segments.s148-g148: This continues the predicate about the same person: 'نگهبان' (guardian/protector) as predicate noun, followed by two coordinated objects 'هر مرز و هر کشورست' (every border and every country). 'هر' is the distributive 'each/every', and the final element 'کشورست' is the fused form of 'کشور است' (is country / country), here functioning as the noun 'country' in the coordinated list.
segments.s148-g149: 'چنین داد پاسخ مر او را هجیر' is arranged in classical word order where the verb 'داد' (gave) precedes or follows its objects and the subject 'هجیر' (Hojir) appears at the end. 'چنین' = 'thus/so'; 'داد' = 'gave'; 'پاسخ' = 'an answer'; 'مر' = 'to me' (dative); 'او را' = 'about him/him' (object/accusative marker 'را' attaches to 'او'); 'هجیر' is the agent (Hojir). The whole line means 'Hojir thus gave me the answer (about him)'.
segments.s148-g150: This is the content clause introduced by 'که' (that). 'شاید' marks conjecture ('perhaps'); 'بدن' = 'body'; 'کان' here = 'as if' (classical particle meaning 'as though'); 'گو' functions like 'saying/so-called' or 'as if to say' (part of the idiom 'کان‌گو' meaning 'as if'); and 'شیرگیر' is the descriptive noun 'lion-catcher' (a bold, lion-hunting man). The clause reads '[...] that perhaps the body is, as it were, of a lion-catcher.'
segments.s148-g151: 'کنون' = now; 'رفته' = gone (past participle used with an auxiliary to express perfect or conjectural past); 'باشد' here is an auxiliary/modal marker implying conjecture ('may have gone' / 'must have gone') — in classical Persian 'رفته باشد' expresses probability; 'به' = 'to' (directional); 'زابلستان' = Zabulistan (place name). The sequence expresses conjecture about motion to a place.
segments.s148-g152: 'که' here introduces an explanatory clause ('for/since'); 'هنگام' = 'the time/season'; 'بزمست' = 'it is a time of festivity' (predicate nominal indicating a feast/revel); 'در' = 'in'; 'گلستان' = 'the rose-garden' (a literal garden or figurative courtly garden). Together: 'for it is the time of revelry in the garden.'
segments.s148-g49: سراپرده‌ای is an indefinite noun (suffixed -ای) meaning an inner curtain/tent; it is the thing being described. بر کشیده is a past participle construction: کشیده (drawn/pulled) with the preposition/particle بر preceding it, so the phrase means ‘drawn up/raised over.’ سیاه is an adjective (postpositive here, as typical in Persian poetry) modifying سراپرده‌ای: ‘black.’
segments.s148-g50: زده is a past participle used adjectivally (here qualifying گردش) — classical Persian often places participles before the noun they modify; گردش means a circuit/turn. اندر ز هر سو is a prepositional phrase ‘in/from every side’ (اندر = in/within; ز = from; هر سو = every side). سپاه is the noun ‘host/army’ and is the entity arranged in that circuit.
segments.s148-g51: به گرد اندرش = ‘around it’ (به گرد = around; اندرش = اندر + ـش, ‘in/around it’ with the possessive/proximate suffix). خیمه is the noun ‘tents.’ ز اندازه بیش is an idiomatic phrase: ز + اندازه + بیش = ‘beyond measure’ / ‘more than can be measured,’ so the tents are described as innumerable or excessive in size/number.
segments.s148-g52: This short clause pairs spatial terms around the elephants: پس پشت پیلان = ‘behind (were) the elephants’ backs’ (پس used here in a spatial/temporal sense ‘after/behind’; پشت = rear/back; پیلان = elephants). و بالاش پیش = ‘and before it their fronts’ (بالا + ـش = its/that one’s upper/front part; پیش = before). The possessive suffix ـش on بالا refers back to the tent/center.
segments.s148-g53: زده (past participle of زدن) functions adjectivally/verbally as ‘set/raised’ and is placed before the phrase it affects; پیش او = ‘before him/in his presence.’ پیل پیکر is a compound adjective meaning ‘elephant‑bodied’ or ‘elephant‑shaped’ and modifies درفش (the banner/standard), so درفش is the noun ‘standard’ described as elephant‑shaped.
segments.s148-g54: به در is a locative phrase ‘at the gate’ (به + در). The verbless clause then presents سواران (horsemen) as standing there; بر here is functioning as a simple prepositional/contact particle in the sense ‘before/at,’ linking the location and the people. زرینه modifies کفش: ‘golden shoes’ — i.e. the horsemen wore golden footwear.
segments.s148-g55: چنین گفت is a standard formula ‘thus it was said/it was said thus.’ کان is an archaic conjunction/particle introducing reported content (here = ‘that’). طوس نوذر بود is a simple clause ‘Tūs was Nowzar’ (بود = was; the sequence reports an identity or statement about Tūs).
segments.s148-g56: درفشش is درفش + possessive suffix ـش = ‘his/its standard.’ کجاپیل‌پیکر is a compound adjective (elephant‑bodied / elephant‑formed) modifying درفشش. بود is the past copula ‘was,’ closing the clause.
segments.s148-g129: نشان پدر جست is a compact past clause with the object (نشانِ پدر, “his father's sign/trace”) placed before the verb جست (past of جستن). The subject is omitted (third person) as is common in Persian poetry. و با او نگفت joins with و + a negative past verb: نگفت is negated past (ن‑ + گفت) meaning “did not say / did not speak,” and با او is the prepositional phrase “with him / to him.”
segments.s148-g130: همی داشت is an older/perhaps continuous past construction: همی (an adverb indicating continuous/ongoing action) + داشت (auxiliary) gives the sense of 'kept' or 'was holding.' آن راستی is the direct object 'that truth,' and در نهفت is a locative/adjectival phrase with نهفت (a participial/adjectival form meaning 'hidden') — so the clause means he 'kept that truth hidden inside.'
segments.s148-g131: تو گیتی چه سازی is a second-person, rhetorical clause: تو (you) + گیتی (the world) + چه سازی (چه + سازی — 'what making/do you make?'). سازی here is a deverbal nominal/verb-form meaning 'do/build/make' addressed to 'you.' The following relative clause که خود ساخت‌ست modifies گیتی; note ساخت‌ست is an older/periphrastic perfect/past form with the enclitic -ست.
segments.s148-g132: جهاندار is the subject 'the world-ruler' or sovereign/creator. ازین کار is a prepositional/partitive phrase 'of/from this work/affair' (از + این written as ازین). پرداخت‌ست is an archaic/perfective verbal form (پرداختن in the sense 'to complete / to carry out / to finish'); here it means 'has carried out / has completed' that work.
segments.s148-g133: زمانه is the subject 'the time / the times / fate,' and نبشته is a noun 'writing / writ' (here: the written destiny). دگرگونه is an adjective 'different / otherwise,' and داشت is the past-tense auxiliary 'had' — together: 'the times had written otherwise' (i.e. fate had decreed other than expected).
segments.s148-g134: چنان 'thus / so' introduces the conclusion. کاو here functions as an archaic relative/compound (roughly 'that which / which he') and precedes گذارد 'he placed/put.' The impersonal modal phrase بباید گذاشت uses بباید ('one must / ought to') plus the past-form گذاشت to express obligation ('it must be left / one must let it stand').
segments.s148-g135: دگر باره is a fixed adverbial 'again / once more.' پرسید is the past 'asked.' ازان is the prepositional/partitive 'from that / of that,' so the phrase means 'he asked again of that srefraz (that proud/victorious one).' Note the object is introduced by ازان.
segments.s148-g136: This clause is elliptical (common in poetry): ازان کش is 'that man/that warrior' (کش as an agentive/nominal element), and به دیدار او 'to/for a meeting with him.' بد نیاز is a nominal phrase meaning 'in bad/poor need' = 'sorely in need / desperate.' The main copula/verb (e.g. آمد or بود) is omitted and understood from context: 'that man came to see him in great need.'
segments.s148-g73: برو is an imperative 'go'. هر زمان is a temporal phrase 'whenever/each time'. برخروشد is a 3rd‑person present (classical/fused form of برخاست + شود) meaning 'rises up/comes up' and here has no explicit subject (the horse is implied). همی is the classical continuous/habitual particle that accompanies present verbs (often post‑verb) and gives a sense of recurring action.
segments.s148-g74: تو گویی is a formulaic clause meaning 'you would say' or 'it seems (as if)' — گویی here functions as a present verbal/parenthetical phrase. که introduces the content clause. در زین = 'in the saddle' (در = in; زین = saddle). بجوشد همی is a present form of جوشیدن with همی marking continuous/habitual sense; the verb paints a vivid image 'is boiling/steaming' or 'seems to boil'.
segments.s148-g75: This is a nominal phrase: بسی پیل برگستوان‌دار پیش = 'many/very (?) elephants, broad‑shouldered, before (him)'. برگستوان‌دار is a compound adjective ('having/with broad shoulders'); پیش functions as a locative 'before/in front (of him)'. Note that بسی is a classical intensifier/quantifier and here heads the noun phrase.
segments.s148-g76: همی جوشد is the verb phrase: جوشد is 3rd‑person present of جوشیدن and here means 'seethe/boil (with emotion)'; همی again signals ongoing/habitual action. آن مرد is the subject ('that man') placed after the verb by poetic inversion. بر جای خویش = 'on his place / in his place' where خویش is the possessive form (خوی + ش), so جای خویش = 'his place/spot.'
segments.s148-g77: نه ... constructs a negative clause. مردست here represents the fused sequence 'مرد است' (man + copula 'is'); the sense in context is existential/negative: 'there is no man'. از ایران = 'from Iran'. به بالای اوی = 'above him' or 'superior to him' (به بالای = on/over, اوی = 'him'/'his'). The copula is contracted and word order is classical/poetic.
segments.s148-g78: The second negative half: نه بینم همی = 'nor do I see' (بینم is 1st‑person present 'I see', with نه negating it; همی again marks the continuous/habitual flavor). اسپ همتای اوی = 'a steed equal to him' (همتا = match/counterpart; اوی = 'his/him'). Word order places the object phrase after the negated verb as is common in poetry.
segments.s148-g79: درفشی بدید: درفشی (درفش = banner/standard) is the direct object and بدید is 3rd‑person past 'he saw'. اژدها پیکرست is a predicate clause where پیکرست = 'is (of) shape/body' (پیکر + است fused) so the standard 'was dragon‑shaped' (اژدها = dragon). Note the classical fusion of noun + copula and the verbless descriptive link.
segments.s148-g80: بران = 'upon it/thereon'. نیزه is 'a spear'. بر شیر زرین سرست is a compact description: بر = 'on' (locative relation), شیر زرین = 'golden lion', and سرست = 'is a head' (سر + است fused) — i.e. 'at its tip was a golden lion's head.' The clause uses fused copulas and stacked prepositional relations typical of Ferdowsi's poetic economy.
segments.s148-g217: "که چون برکشد از چمن بیخ سرو" is a temporal/comparative clause: که + چون = "when/that when"; برکشد is a classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of برکشیدن (to pull up/uproot) placed before its complements, and از چمن بیخِ سرو = "from the meadow, the root of the cypress" (بیخ + اسم in genitive). Word order is Verb — prepositional phrase — noun phrase (poetic inversion), so the whole unit means "when he uproots the cypress by its root from the meadow."
segments.s148-g218: "سزد‌گر گیا را نبوید تذرو" begins with the conditional/optative compound سزد گر (= "if it befit / if it is fitting"); گیا را is the definite object marker on 'the plant'; نبوید is the classical negative present/subjunctive/indicative of بوییدن (does not emit / does not give off a scent); تذرو is a poetic present form meaning "when/if scattered" or "is scattered." Together the clause reads roughly "if that be fitting, the plant does not give off scent when scattered." Note the poetic order places the negative verb before the circumstantial verb at the end.
segments.s148-g219: به سهراب گفت = "he said to Sohrab" (به + name + past-tense گفت used as the reporting verb). این چه آشفتنست is an exclamatory verbless/stative clause: این چه + آشفتنست (classical form of آشفته‌است / "is unrest/confusion") = "what is this disorder/confusion?" The older form آشفتنست fuses the adjective/participle with the copula.
segments.s148-g220: همه با من از رستمت گفتنست = "Everyone (is) with me speaking about your Rostam." Here همه is the subject "all/everyone," با من functions as "to me / before me" in the sense "they come/tell me," از رستمت = "about your Rostam" (رستمت = رستم + ت, the possessive/relational form), and گفتنست is an archaic impersonal/present reporting form meaning "they say / it is said." The clause therefore reports that all are telling the speaker about Rostam.
segments.s148-g221: نباید ترا جست با او نبرد reads as an admonition: نباید = "one must not / you should not," ترا = the 2nd-person object form (here marking the person who would act), and جست (classical infinitive/verb of جستن) = "seek/attempt." با او نبرد = "to fight with him" (نبرد = battle). Together the clause admonishes "you should not seek/begin a fight with him." Note the classical ordering and the separation of ترا into ت + را in the manuscript-style segmentation.
segments.s148-g222: برآرد به آوردگاه از تو گرد: برآرد (classical 3rd sing. of برآوردن) = "he will bring/lead forth," به آوردگاه = "to the battlefield," and از تو گرد is a terse poetic phrase meaning "and (will) [turn/drive] you away / put you to rout" — گرد here participates as a verbal/adverbial element indicating motion/turning away. The clause warns that if one fights, he will be brought out to the field and put to rout.
segments.s148-g223: همی پیلتن را نخواهی شکست = همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here best translated "indeed/you will surely"), پیلتن را = "Piltan" with the object marker را, نخواهی is the classical 2nd-person future/auxiliary (you will not / you shall not), and شکست is the bare verb/noun "defeat" serving as the object/complement. The line therefore states "You will not defeat Piltan."
segments.s148-g224: همانا که آسان نیاید به دست = همانا is an emphatic interjection "verily/truly," که introduces the explanatory clause, آسان = "easy," نیاید is the classical negative present of آمدن (does not come), and به دست (literally "to the hand") is an idiom meaning "into one's grasp / into one's power." So the line explains why: "For indeed it does not come easily into one's hand."
segments.s148-g209: چنین گفت موبد که مردن به نام — Literally 'thus said the mobed that dying with a name.' گفت is the past tense 'said' introducing a reported clause headed by که. مردن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to die' (used as the topic) and is followed by the prepositional phrase به نام 'with a name' (= 'with fame'). Word order: speech-verb (چنین گفت موبد) + complementizer که + verb-noun phrase (مردن به نام).
segments.s148-g210: به از زنده دشمن بدو شادکام — به از is an archaic comparative 'better than'. The remainder gives the thing being compared: 'زنده دشمن' = 'a living enemy' and بدو is the dative/enclitic 'to him/about him' (به او → بدو). شادکام functions as predicate/adjective 'happy/pleased' or 'fortunate.' Together: 'better than being alive while the enemy is pleased with him.'
segments.s148-g211: اگر من شوم کشته بر دست اوی — Conditional protasis introduced by اگر 'if'. شوم is the present-subjunctive/optative of شدن used here as 'should I be / I become' (i.e. 'if I am'). کشته is the past participle 'killed/slain' forming the passive. بر دست + pronoun (اوی) literally 'on his hand' = 'by his hand'; اوی here is the older/poetic genitive/dative form 'his'/'by him'.
segments.s148-g212: نگردد سیه‌روز چون آب جوی — نگردد is a negative jussive/optative of گردیدن (here 'become/turn'), meaning 'will not become' or 'shall not turn into' in the result clause. سیه‌روز is a compound adjective/noun 'black-day' → 'ill‑fated, unlucky person' used predicatively. چون introduces a simile 'like', followed by آب جوی 'running/stream water' (آب + جوی).
segments.s148-g213: چو گودرز و هفتاد پور گزین — چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The phrase lists the people: the proper name گودرز followed by و هفتاد پور 'and seventy sons' where پور = 'son' (a patronymic element) and گزین qualifies them as 'chosen/selected' (here an adjectival/participial use). Word order: temporal conjunction + noun phrase (name + numeral + noun + adjective).
segments.s148-g214: همه پهلوانان با آفرین — همه پهلوانان 'all the champions' is the subject noun phrase; با آفرین is an adverbial phrase 'with praise/acclamation' (آفرین as an exclamation/noun). The whole segment indicates that all the champions were present and accompanied the scene with acclamation.
segments.s148-g215: نباشد به ایران تن من مباد — This is an archaic optative/wish formula. نباشد ... مباد together express 'may it not be that ...'. به ایران marks the locative 'in Iran', تن من is 'my body'. The construction therefore means 'may my body not be/remain in Iran.'
segments.s148-g216: چنین دارم از موبد پاک یاد — چنین دارم 'I have thus' = 'this is what I have / I hold this (recollection/opinion)'. از موبد پاک یاد is an ablative phrasing 'a remembrance from the pure mobed' (پاک qualifying موبد). Word order: statement of possession/opinion (چنین دارم) followed by the source of that remembrance (از موبد پاک یاد).
segments.s148-g153: Badū (بدو) is a prepositional pronoun 'to him/to it' placed before the verb (poetic inversion): 'بدو گفت سهراب' = 'to him Sohrab said' (more colloquial: 'Sohrab said to him'). گفت is the 3rd-person past of گفتن here ('said'). کاین introduces the thing being referred to ('this/that'), خود adds emphatic/reflexive force ('yourself' / 'himself'), and مگوی is the negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not say' (مگو in classical Persian). The whole segment is a tag: 'Sohrab said to him, do not say this yourself...'.
segments.s148-g154: که begins a subordinate clause ('that...'). دارد here functions as a simple present 'has/possesses' (or more generally as a present auxiliary meaning 'there is/has'), introducing what is possessed. سپهبد is the noun 'spahbed' (a high military commander) and سوی جنگ is the prepositional phrase 'toward war'. روی in classical poetry can function verbally ('go/turn'), so the clause means roughly 'that (he) has a spahbed who turns/goes toward war.'
segments.s148-g155: به رامش is a locative phrase 'in/into tranquility' (به + noun). نشیند is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of نشستن ('he sits' or optative 'let him sit'); in poetic word order the verb precedes the subject. جهان‌پهلوان is the appositive subject 'the world‑champion' (a title for the hero).
segments.s148-g156: برو here functions as a discourse/imperative particle (literally 'go' but often used poetically to mark a transition: 'then'/'so'); بر is an adverbial/intensifying particle ('forth/over' or prefix-like). بخندند is 3rd-person plural (subjunctive/present) 'they laugh'. پیر و جوان is a fixed expression 'old and young' (meaning people of all ages) and is the subject of بخندند.
segments.s148-g157: This sentence uses a somewhat inverted/condensed classical order: مرا + را together form 'مرا' = 'me' (object), با تو 'with you', امروز 'today', and پیمان is the nominal subject 'pact/covenant'. یکیست ('is one') is the predicate. Literally: 'Between me and you today the pact is one' → 'Today the pact between you and me is one.'
segments.s148-g158: بگوییم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of گفتن here ('let us say' / 'we will say'); the provided lexical senses list for this token is nominal and doesn't match the verb, so it is marked accordingly. و is 'and'. گفتار ما 'our speech' or 'what we say' is the topic, and اندکیست is a nominal predicate meaning 'is little/brief' — 'our speech is brief.'
segments.s148-g159: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. پهلوان را is the direct object with the object marker را ('the champion'). نمایی is a verbal form here meaning 'you show' / 'you display' (2nd-person singular subjunctive/optative or simple present in classical usage). به من is the indirect object 'to me'. The clause reads 'If you show the champion to me...'.
segments.s148-g160: سرافراز is an adjective 'honored/lofty'. باشی is 2nd-person singular subjunctive/potential of بودن ('you shall be' / 'you will be'); together سرافراز باشی expresses the promised result 'you will be honored.' به هر انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'in every assembly' (literally 'to every assembly' but idiomatically 'at every gathering').
segments.s148-g161: ترا is the object 'to you' (here written as the clitic ت plus the object marker را). The clause contains an implicit verb دهم ('I give' / 'I will grant') — in classical Persian دهم (1st singular) expresses the speaker's giving or granting (often with a future/wish sense). بی‌نیازی is a noun 'self-sufficiency / freedom from want' and در جهان is an adverbial phrase 'in the world' that locates the gift.
segments.s148-g162: گشاده کنم is a periphrastic causative/verb phrase: گشاده (open) plus کنم (1st-person present/subjunctive of کردن) = 'I will open / I will cause to be opened.' گنجهای نهان is the object 'the hidden treasures' (گنجهای plural + نهان post-positive adjective). Word order is somewhat flexible/poetic: the verb phrase appears before its object.
segments.s148-g163: ور is a conditional 'if' (or 'or if'); ایدون here has an archaic/hortative sense ('if it comes to pass' / 'if it should happen'), followed by که introducing the content clause. این راز داری ز من literally 'you have this secret from me' = 'you keep this secret from me' (داری = 'you have/hold'). ز is the poetic variant of از 'from'.
segments.s148-g164: گشاده here functions adverbially/adjectivally 'open(ly)' while بپوشی (present subjunctive/imperative form of پوشیدن) means 'you cover / conceal'. The phrase به من بر سخن means 'in speech toward me' (به من = toward me; بر سخن = 'in/on speech'), so the clause says you will 'openly conceal it when speaking to me' — an apparent paradoxical phrasing used to mean disguising or feigning openness while hiding the secret.
segments.s148-g165: سرت را is 'your head' with the object marker را. نخواهد is the negative form of خواستن and here means 'he will not want / he will not accept'; همی is an adverbial particle (emphatic/continuous). تن به جای literally 'the body in its place' or 'the body instead' where به جای = 'in place of / instead of'. So the clause contrasts 'head' and 'body' with a verb of desire/refusal — a compact, somewhat elliptical phrase typical of poetry.
segments.s148-g166: نگر is an imperative 'look, consider.' تا here functions as 'until / so that' introducing a purpose or limit. کدامین is 'which (one)' and به آیدت is a light subordinate construction 'may come to you' (آید + clitic ت = 'comes to you'). رای is noun 'opinion / counsel', so کدامین به آیدت رای = 'which counsel will come to you.'
segments.s148-g167: نبینی is a negative present/interrogative 'do you not see' (classical rhetorical question). که introduces the subordinate clause 'what the mobed said to Khosrow': موبد is the subject (the Zoroastrian priest), به خسرو = 'to Khosrow', چه = 'what', and گفت here functions as the past 'said' (classical past of گفتن); the whole clause asks the listener to recall that utterance.
segments.s148-g168: بدانگه 'thereupon / at that moment' + که 'when' introduces a temporal clause. بگشاد is the perfect/past of گشودن 'he opened/revealed' (used here of revealing a secret), راز = 'the secret', and از نهفت = 'from concealment' (از + نهفت 'hidden, concealed'). Together: 'at that moment when he revealed the secret that had been hidden.'
segments.s148-g193: This clause states the extent of fear. از + noun (آتش) here means 'because of / from' (causal), ت is the 2nd-person enclitic referring to 'you', and را is the emphatic/definite object marker attached after that enclitic. بیم is the noun 'fear' and چندان بود is a predicative phrase 'was so great' (چندان = 'to that extent; so much', بود = past of بودن). Word order is causal phrase → object-marker'd person → head noun → degree → copula.
segments.s148-g194: که introduces a result clause ('that / so that'). دریا is the subject 'the sea.' به آرام here is adverbial ('in calm / calmly')—به takes an adverbial force (by/-ly) and آرام is 'calm.' خندان is a predicate adjective 'smiling,' and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause therefore gives the consequence of the preceding چندان بود.
segments.s148-g195: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' دریای سبز is a nominal phrase 'the green sea' (سبز used imagistically); اندر آید = 'comes in/enters' with اندر + present/past stem آید 'comes.' ز جای = 'from its place' (ز = poetic 'from', جای = 'place'). The clause sets the temporal scene for the following main clause.
segments.s148-g196: ندارد is 'does not have' (نزاع of داشتن in negative form) so the phrase means 'has no ...'. دم here is literal 'breath' but idiomatically 'a moment/rest'; آتش is the subject 'the fire'; تیزپای is an epithet 'swift‑footed' modifying آتش. So the whole means the nimble fire 'has no breath' (i.e., no respite) when the sea comes in.
segments.s148-g197: سر تیرگی is a nominal phrase where سر functions as 'chief/top' (i.e., 'the head/chief of darkness'). اندر آید به خواب = 'enters into sleep' (اندر + آید = 'comes in', به خواب = 'into sleep'). This describes darkness retreating or falling asleep.
segments.s148-g198: چو again = 'when.' تیغ 'sword' + از میان برکشد is a verbal phrase: از میان = 'from the middle/midst' and برکشد (here) is the specific sense 'draws (a weapon)'. آفتاب is the agent 'the sun.' The clause is a simile/temporal image: when the sun 'draws its sword' from between (the clouds/sky), darkness retires.
segments.s148-g199: به دل گفت is an idiom meaning 'said to his heart' = 'he thought to himself' or 'he said inwardly.' به is the dative 'to,' دل is 'heart,' and گفت here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (though the supplied sense list labels noun senses, the grammatical role is verbal past). پس = 'then/afterwards.' کاردیده is an adjective 'experienced/well‑tried' describing هجیر, which is the proper name.
segments.s148-g200: که‌گر (classical fusion of که + گر) is a conditional 'if.' من is the subject 'I.' نشان گو is a verbal phrase: نشان 'signal/sign' and گو (here) functions as the verb 'to say/give' (nishan-go = 'give a signal'); شیرگیر is the goal/intent 'lion‑catcher' (i.e., to catch the lion or to be a lion‑catcher). The whole is a first‑person conditional thought: 'If I give the sign, I will (be able to) catch the lion.'
